title: Neurosurgery Consultation: What to Bring and Expect for Success
metaDescription: Prepare for your first neurosurgery consultation with Prof. Giv Sharifi. Learn what medical records, MRI scans, and questions to bring for your initial visit.
excerpt: Preparing for your first neurosurgery consultation is essential for a successful outcome. Learn what records to bring, what to expect, and how to prepare.

content.text: Introduction to Your Neurosurgery Consultation

Preparing for a neurosurgery consultation is a significant step toward addressing complex neurological or spinal conditions. It is completely natural to feel a mix of emotions, ranging from anxiety to hope, when you first schedule an appointment with a specialist like Prof. Giv Sharifi. Understanding what to expect during this initial meeting can help alleviate your concerns and ensure that you make the most of your time with the medical team. This consultation serves as the foundation for your personalized treatment plan, allowing the surgeon to evaluate your symptoms, review diagnostic imaging, and discuss potential therapeutic pathways. By arriving prepared, you empower yourself to participate actively in your healthcare journey, ensuring that every question you have is addressed thoroughly and professionally.

During this primary visit, the goal is not necessarily to schedule surgery immediately, but rather to establish a clear diagnosis and explore all available options. Prof. Giv Sharifi prioritizes patient education, ensuring that you understand the nature of your condition and the reasoning behind any recommended procedures. Whether you are seeking a first assessment or a second opinion, the consultation process is designed to be collaborative. You will have the opportunity to discuss your medical history in detail, which provides the context necessary for an accurate assessment. This professional dialogue is the first step in building a trusting relationship between you and your neurosurgeon, which is vital for achieving the best possible clinical outcomes.
Gathering Essential Medical Records and Imaging

One of the most critical aspects of your preparation is compiling a comprehensive set of medical records. When you meet with Prof. Giv Sharifi, having organized documentation allows for a more efficient and accurate evaluation of your health status. You should bring any relevant reports from previous doctors, including summaries of past treatments, medications you are currently taking, and a list of any known allergies. If you have undergone previous surgeries, obtaining the operative reports can provide invaluable insights into your surgical history. This level of preparation demonstrates your commitment to your health and allows your neurosurgeon to focus on analyzing your current condition rather than spending time tracking down missing information.

Imaging studies are perhaps the most important documents you will bring to your consultation. If you have had a brain MRI or a spine MRI, it is essential to bring the actual digital images on a disc or via a secure cloud link, rather than just the written report. The radiologist’s report provides a summary, but Prof. Giv Sharifi needs to personally review the raw images to understand the nuances of your anatomy and the specific details of your condition. Having your most recent scans available ensures that the assessment is based on the most current information, which is crucial for determining the most appropriate and effective treatment plan for your specific neurological needs.
The Importance of Seeking a Second Opinion

Many patients feel hesitant about seeking a second opinion, but it is a standard and encouraged practice in the field of neurosurgery. A second opinion can provide peace of mind, confirm a diagnosis, or introduce alternative treatment perspectives that you may not have considered previously. Prof. Giv Sharifi welcomes patients who are seeking further clarity regarding their neurological or spinal health. By reviewing your case with a fresh set of eyes, your surgeon can ensure that all potential non-invasive or minimally invasive options have been fully explored before recommending more intensive interventions. This process is about ensuring you have the highest confidence in your chosen treatment path.

When you come for a second opinion, please bring all the information you received from your previous consultations. This includes any proposed surgical plans or diagnostic conclusions reached by other specialists. Prof. Giv Sharifi will carefully review these findings alongside your current medical status to provide an independent and expert evaluation. This collaborative approach helps to clarify complex medical situations and ensures that you are fully informed about the risks, benefits, and expected outcomes of any proposed procedures. Ultimately, seeking a second opinion is a proactive step that puts your long-term health and well-being at the center of the decision-making process.
Preparing Your Questions for the Surgeon

Active participation is key to a successful consultation, and preparing a list of questions beforehand is highly recommended. It is easy to forget important points when you are in the office, so writing down your concerns ensures that nothing is overlooked. You might want to ask about the specific nature of your condition, the potential progression if left untreated, and the various treatment modalities available. Understanding the goals of any proposed surgery, such as symptom relief or preventing further neurological decline, is essential. Prof. Giv Sharifi encourages patients to be inquisitive, as clear communication is the cornerstone of effective neurosurgical care and helps manage expectations throughout your recovery journey.

Beyond the medical details, you should also feel comfortable asking about the logistics of your potential care. Inquire about the recovery timeline, the expected duration of your hospital stay, and any necessary lifestyle adjustments you might need to make post-surgery. You may also want to ask about the surgeon’s experience with your specific condition and the frequency with which they perform the recommended procedure. These questions help you build a comprehensive picture of what your life will look like during and after treatment. Remember that there are no unimportant questions; your surgeon is there to provide the clarity and support you need to feel confident in your medical decisions.
What Happens During the Physical Examination

The physical and neurological examination is a fundamental part of your consultation. Prof. Giv Sharifi will perform a series of tests to assess your motor strength, sensory perception, reflexes, and coordination. This examination helps the surgeon correlate your reported symptoms with objective findings, which is vital for localizing the source of your neurological issue. For example, if you are experiencing back pain, the exam will focus on identifying which spinal nerves may be affected. This hands-on evaluation provides critical data that imaging alone cannot capture, allowing for a more holistic understanding of how your condition is impacting your daily physical function.

During the exam, you may be asked to perform simple movements, walk in specific ways, or respond to various sensory stimuli. These tests are non-invasive and are designed to provide a baseline for your current neurological status. Prof. Giv Sharifi will explain each step of the process, ensuring you feel comfortable and informed throughout the examination. The findings from this physical assessment will be integrated with your medical history and imaging results to form a complete clinical picture. This thoroughness is a hallmark of high-quality neurosurgical practice and ensures that the diagnosis is as precise as possible before any treatment recommendations are finalized.
Understanding Potential Treatment Pathways

Once the evaluation is complete, Prof. Giv Sharifi will discuss the potential treatment pathways available to you. It is important to understand that surgery is not always the first or only option. Depending on your diagnosis, your surgeon may recommend a period of observation, physical therapy, medication management, or other non-invasive interventions. If surgery is deemed necessary, your surgeon will explain the specific techniques involved, such as minimally invasive approaches, which are often used to reduce recovery time and minimize tissue trauma. You will be given a clear explanation of why a particular path is recommended and what the expected outcomes may be for your specific case.

Your surgeon will also discuss the risks and benefits associated with any proposed treatment. Informed consent is a vital part of the process, and you will have ample time to process this information and ask follow-up questions. Prof. Giv Sharifi believes in a patient-centered approach where you are an active partner in your care. By understanding the rationale behind your treatment plan, you can approach your surgery or therapy with a sense of control and confidence. This transparency ensures that you are fully aware of what to expect during the healing process and helps set realistic goals for your recovery and long-term neurological health.

Frequently Asked Questions
Do I need a referral to see Prof. Giv Sharifi?

While a referral from your primary care physician or another specialist is often helpful for coordinating your care, it is not always strictly required. You can contact the office directly to schedule an initial consultation. Providing your medical history and any relevant imaging reports during the booking process can help the team prepare for your visit. If you have a referral, please bring it with you, as it often contains important information regarding your symptoms and previous diagnostic efforts. Our team is dedicated to making the scheduling process as smooth and accessible as possible for all patients.
How long does the first consultation usually take?

The duration of your first consultation can vary depending on the complexity of your condition and the amount of medical history that needs to be reviewed. Generally, you should plan for the appointment to last between forty-five minutes to an hour. This allows sufficient time for a thorough review of your imaging, a comprehensive physical examination, and a detailed discussion regarding your diagnosis and potential treatment options. We prioritize quality time with each patient to ensure that all your concerns are addressed without feeling rushed, as this is essential for providing excellent neurosurgical care.
Can I bring a family member or friend with me?

We strongly encourage you to bring a family member, friend, or caregiver to your consultation. Having an extra set of ears can be very helpful when discussing complex medical information, as they can help you remember details and ask questions you might have forgotten. Your support person can also provide emotional comfort during what can be a stressful time. Please feel free to have them join you in the examination room so that everyone involved in your care journey is on the same page regarding your diagnosis and the proposed treatment plan.
What should I do if my MRI scans are on a film?

While digital formats are preferred for modern neurosurgical planning, we can certainly review traditional film-based MRI scans if that is what you have available. However, we highly recommend that you visit the imaging center where the scans were performed and request a digital copy on a CD or via a secure online portal. Digital images allow Prof. Giv Sharifi to manipulate the views and zoom in on specific areas, which provides a much higher level of detail than physical film. If you only have film, please bring it, but digital copies will always provide the best results.
Will I be scheduled for surgery immediately after the visit?

It is very rare for surgery to be scheduled immediately following an initial consultation. The primary goal of your first visit is to establish an accurate diagnosis and discuss all viable treatment options. If surgery is determined to be the most appropriate course of action, we will provide you with all the necessary information to make an informed decision. You will have time to consider the recommendations, discuss them with your family, and ask any follow-up questions before moving forward. We believe in a thoughtful, deliberate approach to surgical planning to ensure the best possible safety and outcomes for our patients.
